text: The surgeon performs colonic and rectal dissection for a panproctocolectomy, starting with the lateral peritoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon. The dissection progresses laterally towards the splenic flexure, using medial retraction to aid in the lateral dissection of the colon. Medial dissection defines the pedicle, which is then divided using a laparoscopic stapler. The dissection continues medially towards the inferior mesenteric vein and laterally around the splenic flexure. The anterior leaf of the omentum is detached from the transverse colon.

The surgeon performs colonic and rectal dissection for a panproctocolectomy, starting with the lateral peritoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon. The dissection progresses laterally towards the splenic flexure, with medial retraction aiding the process. Medial dissection defines the pedicle, which is then divided using a laparoscopic stapler. Further medial dissection targets the inferior mesenteric vein, while additional lateral dissection approaches the splenic flexure.

The surgeon removes the anterior leaf of the omentum from the transverse colon, progressing the dissection along the transverse colon by alternating between separating omental attachments and mesentery.

The surgeon continues dissection along the transverse colon, alternating between releasing omental attachments and mesentery. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure, where superior attachments are released. After releasing the hepatic flexure, the dissection shifts to the lateral aspect of the right colon, moving inferiorly with the aid of medial retraction.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the hepatic flexure, releasing attachments superiorly. After releasing the hepatic flexure, the dissection shifts to the lateral aspect of the right colon inferiorly, using medial retraction. The peritoneal attachments are released, and the vessel is isolated and divided with a laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon releases the peritoneal attachments and isolates the vessel, dividing it with a laparoscopic stapler. The mobility of the colon is assessed. The small bowel mesentery is divided up to the bowel wall, and the small bowel is transected using the laparoscopic stapler.
The surgeon checks the mobility of the colon, then proceeds to divide the small bowel mesentery up to the bowel wall. The small bowel is subsequently divided using a laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon continues rectal dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, progressing posteriorly towards the pelvic floor. Dissection is performed circumferentially, with further posterior dissection towards the pelvic floor. The anterior peritoneal fold is visibly divided.

The surgeon continues rectal dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, progressing posteriorly towards the pelvic floor. The dissection is performed circumferentially, with further posterior dissection towards the pelvic floor. The anterior peritoneal fold is visibly divided.

The surgeon joins the lateral dissection planes anteriorly, continuing circumferential dissection down to the pelvic floor. Following the completion of internal dissection, external intersphincteric dissection is performed, and the specimen is delivered.

The surgeon joins the lateral dissection planes anteriorly and continues circumferential dissection down to the pelvic floor. Following the completion of internal dissection, external intersphincteric dissection is performed, and the specimen is delivered.
